--- SOURCE 3: Host-Pathogen Interactions: Basic Concepts of Microbial ... ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/

SUMMARY:
Host-Pathogen Interactions: Basic Concepts of Microbial Commensalism, Colonization, Infection, and Disease - PMC  Host-Pathogen Interactions: Basic Concepts of Microbial Commensalism, Colonization, Infection, and Disease Here, we critically review the origin and historical evolution of key concepts used to describe the outcome of host-microbe interactions, namely, infection, commensalism, colonization, persistence, infection, and disease. Although the concept of the carrier state may have undermined the pathogen-centered view of microbial pathogenesis, it invoked a mutability that paved the way for defining host-microbe interaction as a regulated relationship. Thus, rather than providing definitions based on the outcome of host-microbe interaction, the current lexicon puts forth terms that are primarily intended to define a pathogen and whether or not it causes infection and/or disease.

Host-Pathogen Interactions: Basic Concepts of Microbial Commensalism, Colonization, Infection, and Disease
==========================================================================================================

[Arturo Casadevall](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Casadevall%20A%22%5BAuthor%5D)

### Arturo Casadevall

1 Division of Infectious Diseases, Department of Medicine, and Department of Microbiology, Albert Einstein College of Medicine, Bronx, New York 10461

Find articles by [Arturo Casadevall](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Casadevall%20A%22%5BAuthor%5D)

1,*, [Liise-anne Pirofski](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Pirofski%20LA%22%5BAuthor%5D)

### Liise-anne Pirofski

1 Division of Infectious Diseases, Department of Medicine, and Department of Microbiology, Albert Einstein College of Medicine, Bronx, New York 10461

Find articles by [Liise-anne Pirofski](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Pirofski%20LA%22%5BAuthor%5D)

1

Editor: D A Portnoy 1

*   Author information
*   Copyright and License information

1 Division of Infectious Diseases, Department of Medicine, and Department of Microbiology, Albert Einstein College of Medicine, Bronx, New York 10461

*
Corresponding author. Mailing address: Department of Medicine, 1300 Morris Park Ave., Bronx, NY 10461. Phone: (718) 430-4259. Fax: (718) 430-8968. E-mail: casadeva@aecom.yu.edu.

Copyright © 2000, American Society for Microbiology

[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)

PMCID: PMC97744 PMID: [11083759](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11083759/)

* * *

Most of the terminology used to define the host-microbe interaction has been in use for nearly a century. Early in this period, microbes were thought to be primary aggressors that governed the host-pathogen interaction, resulting in disease. Later, new information about the attributes of microbes and their hosts resulted in the understanding that the host-pathogen interaction does not always result in disease. This recognition, in turn, led to the introduction of terms to explain states in which microbes exist within hosts without causing overt disease and why some microbes only cause disease in certain hosts. Commensal, carrier state, and opportunist were terms put forth to account for microbes and conditions that were sometimes associated with disease but for which Koch's postulates could not be fulfilled for one reason or another. Most of these terms were originally proposed to describe the behavior of particular microbes, rather than to define a more general host-microbe relationship.

Recently, we reviewed the concepts of virulence and pathogenicity and described how the definitions for these terms changed over the years as microbiologists tried to find ways to convey that microbial pathogenesis reflects an interaction between two entities, host and pathogen ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B7)). Based on the concept that host damage was the most relevant outcome of the host-pathogen interaction, we proposed revisions to the definitions of the terms pathogen, pathogenicity, and virulence ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B7)). However, the proposed framework suggested a need to reexamine the terms used to define the outcomes of host-microbe interactions. Here, we critically review the origin and historical evolution of key concepts used to describe the outcome of host-microbe interactions, namely, infection, commensalism, colonization, persistence, infection, and disease. We propose that the meaning of these terms can be clarified by placing them in the context of the damage framework put forth previously ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B7)).

LEXICON OF MICROBIAL PATHOGENESIS
---------------------------------

Once the germ theory of disease was accepted, microbes were considered to be pathogens if they met the stipulations of Koch's postulate. However, it rapidly became apparent that (i) although there are many microbes, most human infections were caused by only a few; (ii) some microbes were classified as pathogens although they did not cause disease in every host; and (iii) some microbes were classified as nonpathogens, although they did cause disease in certain hosts (for an early review, see reference [56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B56)). In addition, it became evident that normal individuals harbored, in their mouth, gut, and skin, large numbers of microbes that did not cause disease. New ideas and terminology, heretofore referred to collectively as a lexicon, were devised to accommodate this information.

By the early twentieth century, it was apparent that pathogenicity was neither an invariant nor a stable characteristic of most microbes and that the acquisition of pathogenic microbes was not necessarily synonymous with disease. In the laboratory, the successful attenuation of pathogens revealed that virulence could be increased or decreased by animal passage and/or in vitro culture (for a review of early experimentation, see reference [4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B4)). This scientific advance eventually led to the development of vaccines that controlled many of the major childhood diseases of the past. In the clinical realm, it was recognized that a microbe responsible for an epidemic disease could be isolated from both symptomatic and asymptomatic individuals in the midst of an epidemic. For example, Henrici noted that only a small number of individuals developed disease when epidemics of cerebrospinal meningitis (_Neisseria meningiditis_) occurred in a community, whereas others carried the bacteria but remained healthy (i.e., carriers); however, the majority were neither sick nor carriers ([18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B18)). Based on the ability to culture staphylococci and streptococci from most people despite the absence of any manifestation of disease, Kolmer proposed a condition called subinfection ([26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B26)). This concept blurred the distinction between pathogens and nonpathogens and challenged a prevailing concept of microbial pathogenesis that was based on Koch's postulate. The recognition of the carrier state was problematic vis a vis the terminology of the time, because a necessary condition for associating certain pathogens with particular diseases was that the converse of Koch's postulate should be fulfilled; the causative microbe should not be found in unaffected individuals ([55](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B55)). However, the carrier state had not been accounted for in the formulation of Koch's postulate, and the recovery of pathogens from healthy hosts challenged the second part of the postulate that a parasite “occurs in no other disease as a fortuitous and nonpathogenic parasite” (postulate wording as suggested by Evans [[12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B12)]).

The description of the carrier state confused existing definitions of pathogens. To both explain the carrier state and preserve the distinction between pathogenic and nonpathogenic microbes, the concept that host and pathogen could adapt to one another was put forth. Karsner and Ecker described this adaptation as involving changes to both host and microbe, such that the host suffered no damage and the microbe was resistant to the immune system ([25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B25)). Similarly, Park and Williams described the carrier state as a commensal development by the pathogenic microorganism ([36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B36)). Later, the view emerged that the carrier state was transient and true pathogens elicited immune responses that eliminated the pathogens, whereas commensals did not ([54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B54)). Asymptomatic carriage of pathogens that elicited immunity was considered to be a benefit to the host that was tolerated at the risk of serious disease ([54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B54)). Conversely, the recognition that a carrier state could follow resolution of clinical disease, e.g., for _Salmonella enterica_ serovar Typhi, illustrated that in certain hosts there was a persistent risk of transmission, and possibly reacquisition, of infection. Another variation of the carrier state with great clinical importance is “microbial persistence,” which refers to the situation in which susceptible pathogens are not eradicated despite antimicrobial therapy ([31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B31)). The carrier state remains poorly understood, and, as noted by Smith, this area of study is relatively underrepresented in microbial pathogenesis research ([41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B41)). Although the concept of the carrier state may have undermined the pathogen-centered view of microbial pathogenesis, it invoked a mutability that paved the way for defining host-microbe interaction as a regulated relationship.

Intrinsic to the terms commensalism, colonization, and the carrier state was the concept that some microbes had the capacity to persist in their hosts. According to a model proposed by Blaser ([3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B3)), ongoing interactions between host and microbe have different outcomes that depend upon regulation of the host-microbe relationship and coevolution of host and microbe favors an outcome in which the cost of eliminating the microbe is high. In the late twentieth century, studies of bacterial pathogenesis led to the identification of molecular differences between pathogenic and nonpathogenic microbes, and this engendered the belief that pathogens and nonpathogens were intrinsically different ([14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B14), [15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B15), [17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B17), [41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B41)). In this regard, historical definitions of terms used in the field of microbial pathogenesis have focused on distinctions between pathogens and nonpathogens rather than on the frequently divergent outcomes that often characterize different host-microbe relationships. The existence of the latter underscores the need for terminology that describes host-microbe interaction, rather than pathogen-specific characteristics.

Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#T1) lists historical definitions for terms commonly used in the field of microbial pathogenesis. These definitions reveal a conceptual evolution that paralleled emerging concepts in microbial pathogenesis and clinical infectious diseases, albeit in some conflict with current concepts of infection and immunity. For example, the implication that states of colonization or commensalism do not invoke an immune response are inconsistent with evidence that normal microflora can elicit specific antibody responses (reviewed in reference [46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B46)). In fact, antibodies to _Staphylococcus aureus_ and _Candida albicans_ are frequent in healthy individuals that harbor these microbes without disease ([16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B16), [38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B38), [39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B39)), and carriers of group A _Streptococcus_ often have titers of antibody to streptococcal antigens which are attributed to prior infection ([47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B47)). Moreover, the presence of antibodies can represent outcomes as diverse as ongoing viral replication (e.g., in human immunodeficiency virus [HIV] natural or vaccine-elicited immunity, latency, carriage, and cross-reactivity with antigens of another microbe or an unknown antigen. However, if the presence of antibodies implies present or past infection, the view that certain states (e.g., colonization and the carrier state) are precursors to infection requires modification. Thus, rather than providing definitions based on the outcome of host-microbe interaction, the current lexicon puts forth terms that are primarily intended to define a pathogen and whether or not it causes infection and/or disease.

### TABLE 1.

Some historical definitions in the field of microbial pathogenesis[a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#TF1-a)

| Term | Definition[b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#TF1-b) | Reference |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Carrier state | The retained invader (e.g., microbe)—under the influence of the immune environment—gradually dissociates into a saprophytic state | [57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B57) |
|  | A state of animal adaptation whereby the microbe and its products cause no damage; in this state the “organisms themselves have probably developed a state of resistance against substances which ordinarily would destroy the organisms and neutralize their products” | [25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B25) |
|  | When an organism of relatively high pathogenicity may appear in the normal flora without causing disease | [44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B44) |
|  | Certain individuals may continue to harbor a pathogen after clinical recovery from an infectious disease and may serve as carriers of infection | [35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B35) |
| Colonization | An agent is considered to colonize a host when its presence in that host does not cause a specific immune response or infection | [34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B34) |
|  | Microorganisms which do not belong to the normal flora of the host but do not inflict local damage to the host | [51](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B51) |
|  | The appearance or increase in numbers of a particular invasive bacterial species in the resident microflora | [52](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B52) |
|  | Implantation of a microbe at a site, such as multiplication of staphylococci in the anterior nares | [24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B24) |
|  | Multiplication of an organism on a body surface without evoking an immune response | [13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B13) |
| Commensal | A harmless parasite | [36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B36) |
|  | The organisms of the normal flora | [44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B44) |
|  | Microbes that can establish themselves in the throat, nose, or intestines without damage to the host | [53](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B53) |
|  | Nonpathogenic organisms present in varying numbers at sites of the normal host's body that are in contact with the environment | [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B1) |
|  | An organism which “eats at the same table” as another of a different species but which confers on the latter neither benefit nor harm | [35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B35) |
|  | Pertaining to or characterized by commensalism; an organism participating in commensalism | [28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B28) |
| Commensalism | The mutual but almost inconsequential association between bacteria and higher organisms | [57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B57) |
|  | The presence of microorganisms on skin and mucous membranes | [33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B33) |
|  | A symbiotic association between host and microorganism in which the microorganism is benefitted but the host is neither helped nor harmed | [32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B32) |
|  | An organism that lives in close association with another of a different species without either harming or benefitting it | [23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B23) |
|  | A form of parasitism in which no injury is dealt to either participant by the other | [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B19) |
|  | The ability (of a microorganism) to live on the external or internal surfaces of the body without causing disease | [48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B48) |
|  | A symbiotic relationship in which one species derives benefit and the other is unharmed | [28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B28) |
| Germ carrier or carrier | A person who harbors and releases pathogenic organisms without manifesting symptoms of the disease associated with the pathogen | [24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B24) |
| A host that harbors a pathogenic organism in a commensal state | [36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B36) |
|  | Healthy individuals who harbor in their body parasitic organisms which are harmful to others | [18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B18) |
|  | Referred to as subinfection, the state whereby a microbe is intimately associated with and has its normal habitat in a certain part of the body and does no harm until special conditions arise, when it may rapidly invade the tissues and produce infection | [26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B26) |
|  | A carrier is a person, animal, or arthropod who harbors a specific infectious agent in the absence of clinical illness with or without a detectable immune response | [13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B13) |
| Infection | The invasion of the body tissues by microorganisms resulting in disease | [18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B18) |
|  | When microparasites have passed the normal barriers of the skin or mucous membranes and have invaded and proliferated in the deeper tissues | [26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B26) |
|  | A process in which an organism enters, establishes itself, and multiplies in the host (not in others) | [32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B32) |
|  | Invasion of the body by harmful organisms (pathogens), such as bacteria, fungi, protozoa, rickettsiae, or viruses | [23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B23) |
|  | The process whereby pathogenic organisms become established and multiply in or on the body of the host | [24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B24) |
|  | The deposition, colonization, and multiplication of a microorganism in a host; usually accompanied by a host response | [13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B13) |
|  | Invasion of the body with organisms that have the potential to cause disease | [28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B28) |
| Infestation | Distinct from infection in that it applies specifically to animal parasites of macroscopic size, such as intestinal worms | [26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B26) |
| Infectious disease | The result of parasitism in which no mutual adaptation has taken place and in which the invasion of the host by the parasite is marked by a struggle, the local and systemic manifestations of which constitute the disease | [56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B56) |
|  | The abnormal state resulting from the deleterious local and general interaction between a host and an invading parasite, with consequent tissue changes and symptoms | [26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B26) |
|  | The manifestations of the fight between the disease-producing or pathogenic organisms and the host with all its defense mechanisms | [33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B33) |
|  | Infection that becomes apparent | [32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B32) |
| Mutualism | A relation between two dissimilar organisms in which both are benefitted | [32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B32) |
|  | Commensalism in which the relationship is mutually beneficial | [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B19) |
| Opportunist or opportunistic | Microbes which cause no overt clinical or pathological conditions in the normal state but can become invasive when the defenses are disturbed | [37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B37) |
| Pathogens which attack persons with compromised immune function | [35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B35) |
|  | These infections represent the colonization of normally sterile tissues by bacteria from tissues that always support autochthonous populations, and because these autochthonous organisms are well adapted for survival on other tissue surfaces of the same animal, their control and clearance poses a whole spectrum of unique problems | [8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B8) |
|  | Pathogen not able to cause disease in healthy hosts but only in those with impaired defense mechanisms | [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B1) |
|  | Normally harmless organisms which take the opportunity afforded by lowered host resistance to act as pathogens | [48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B48) |

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/table/T1/)

a
Not a complete list. Definitions are representative of the variable definitions used for these terms encountered in the literature.

b
In most cases the definition was taken verbatim from the source stated. However, the wording of some sources was modified to construct a definition based on the meaning implied as understood by the authors.

In summary, practically all past treatises on the subject of host-microbe interaction made some distinction between colonization, infection, and disease. However, the basis for the definitions of these terms was embedded in examples of specific microbes rather than in a general framework. The lack of such a framework has led to some ambiguity in the meaning of these terms.

IMPACT OF CHANGING SPECTRUM OF INFECTIOUS DISEASES ON LEXICON
-------------------------------------------------------------

Though many historical definitions (Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#T1)) are adequate when considered in the context of specific microbes and diseases, most definitions do not account for the varied outcomes of the host-microbe interaction. Early definitions were formulated following the rapid acquisition of new knowledge in the late nineteenth century. At that time, the distinction between pathogenic, nonpathogenic, and commensal organisms may have been more clear-cut, since the majority of human hosts probably had what would be considered normal immunity today, as those with immune impairment were unlikely to survive childhood. The adjective classical has been applied to pathogens that were major causes of infectious disease in the past ([54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B54)). However, the introduction of sanitation, serum therapy, vaccination, and then effective antimicrobial therapy reduced the prevalence of and mortality from classical pathogens, though such microbes remain a major health problem in underdeveloped regions. By the 1950s, neoplasia and inflammatory and degenerative diseases, rather than infectious diseases, were thought to be the major medical problems in industrialized nations. However, the development of corticosteroid and cytotoxic therapies, organ transplantation, invasive surgeries, and ultimately the catastrophe of the HIV epidemic, produced a new population of human hosts with impaired immune systems ([27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B27), [51](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B51)) that were vulnerable to infections with various microbes previously thought to be nonpathogenic ([2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B2)).

A major change in the prevalence of certain pathogens occurred in the twentieth century. This was exemplified by the shift in the etiologic agents of bloodstream infections from gram-positive to gram-negative microbes in the early part of the century, and then back again to gram-positive and fungal microbes toward the end of the century ([11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B11), [14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B14)). The primary cause of these shifts was antibiotic selection. Since multiple microbes may be present in the hospitalized setting, infection represents an outcome of selective pressures in the context of the host-microbe relationship. Unexpected outcomes of the host-microbe relationship are best illustrated by the increased prevalence of unusual infections in individuals with advanced HIV infection. For example, by the middle of the first decade of the HIV epidemic in the early 1990s, _Cryptococcus neoformans_ was the most frequent cause of meningitis in New York City, when the more than 1,000 cases of cryptococcal meningitis ([9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B9)) outnumbered the 285 cases of meningitis caused by all bacterial pathogens ([29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B29)). This reflects the influence of the HIV epidemic upon the spectrum of infectious diseases in a population as well as the impact of the introduction of an effective vaccine against an important pathogen, namely, _Haemophilus influenzae_ type b.

The recognition that microbes thought to be nonpathogens caused disease in certain hosts challenged the definitions of saprophyte and commensal. As a result, additional terms were added to the lexicon in an attempt to find terminology that could accommodate the new medical and scientific findings (Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#T1)). In addition to the terms listed in Table [1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#T1), other terms and adjectives used to describe microbes and their interactions with the host in the literature include pure saprophytes ([57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B57)), pure parasite ([57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B57)), half parasite ([57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B57)), classical ([54](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B54)), persister ([31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B31)), nosocomial ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B1)), iatrogenic ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B1)), convalescent carrier ([48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B48)), precocious carrier ([48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC97744/#B48)), chronic carrier ([48](ht

--- SOURCE 4: Host-Pathogen Interaction - an overview | ScienceDirect Topics ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/host-pathogen-interaction

SUMMARY:
Infection by viruses in humans, organic pathogens infecting the host, and molecular interactions between pathogen proteins and receptors in human cells are all examples of host–pathogen interactions. We discuss the computational and biological features of host–pathogen interactions, as well as categorizing the ways of how pathogens interact with their hosts, various machine learning algorithms currently being utilized for the prediction of host–pathogen interactions, and probable future avenues for this field of study in this chapter. Genetic studies of host–pathogen interactions have tremendously evolved from single-gene perspectives to genome-wide approaches interrogating whole genomes of hosts and pathogens. Host and pathogen-associated microbial communities are being implicated in complex host–pathogen interactions causing successful pathogen infection and disease development.

FULL CONTENT:

Host-Pathogen Interaction
=========================

In subject area:[Agricultural and Biological Sciences](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences)

Aptly, host–pathogen interactions can be described as how microbes or viruses survive within host organisms at the molecular, cellular, organismal, or population levels.

From:[Genomic Surveillance and Pandemic Preparedness, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780443187698000015)

Chapters and Articles
---------------------

You might find these chapters and articles relevant to this topic.

Chapter

[Computational means to understand and elucidate host–pathogen interaction and outcome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780443187698000143)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2023, Genomic Surveillance and Pandemic Preparedness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780443187698/genomic-surveillance-and-pandemic-preparedness)Preeti Agarwal, ... Jitendra Narayan

### Introduction

The interaction between a pathogen and its host is referred to as “host–pathogen interaction.” Pathogens adapt to changes by developing fresh methods of infection and host survival. They are infectious agents that, when the host's [immune system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/immune-system "Learn more about immune system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is unable to protect against them, create disease in the host's body. It can address issues about how viruses enter hosts and how they remain in hosts that have been repeatedly treated or immunized against the same virus. Infection by viruses in humans, organic pathogens infecting the host, and [molecular interactions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/molecular-interaction "Learn more about molecular interactions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") between pathogen proteins and receptors in [human cells](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/human-cell "Learn more about human cells from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are all examples of host–pathogen interactions. We briefly review the history of this field before delving into the methodological aspects of host–pathogen interaction processes to comprehend how and why recent advancements have taken place.

Early studies in host–pathogen interactions include their evaluation of _[Salmonella typhimurium](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/salmonella-enterica-serovar-typhimurium "Learn more about Salmonella typhimurium from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ induced typhoid in mice (Zelle, no date) and understanding the physiology of parasitism in _[Puccinia sorghi](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/puccinia-sorghi "Learn more about Puccinia sorghi from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_, the pathogen responsible for crop rot in plants, through [genetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/genetics "Learn more about genetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") investigation (Dickson et al., 1959). [Pathogenicity](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/pathogenicity "Learn more about Pathogenicity from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and virulence, two fundamental concepts, have been a focus in recent research, that describes and suggests a system for classifying microbial pathogens based on their ability and the degree of damage they can cause to the host as a result of the immune response (Dickson et al., 1959; Casadevall and Pirofski, 1999). Host–pathogen interactions in the same host tissue have been recently investigated in connection to [epigenetics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/epigenomics "Learn more about epigenetics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Gómez-Díaz et al., 2012), metallobiology (Botella et al., 2012), quantitative temporal viromics (Weekes et al., 2014), heterogeneity, and computational systems biology (Bumann, 2015). All of these findings show an indirect extension of the field of host–pathogen interaction study. Rare investigations of infections and their interactions with hosts sparked the discipline. Early research on the interactions between hosts and pathogens mostly focused on environmental elements including light, temperature, season, and pathogen/host population. The identification of several secretion systems laid the groundwork for further research into host–pathogen interactions(Pukatzki et al., 2006). The field's emphasis has changed as a result of the development of protein–protein interactions (PPI) prediction algorithms (Bock and Gough, 2001), which has increased the possibility of identifying host-pathogen protein pairings and their interactions. The ability of pathogens to cling to host tissues, get past the host's defenses and enter host cells. Additional research, however, has revealed that every illness has its unique variation of these patterns (Rupp and Sohn, 2011). Proteins and genes play a major role in host–pathogen interaction, as well as in the production of biofilms, the inhibition of macrophage activity, and other processes. We briefly discussed a number of potential factors in this chapter that might contribute to host–pathogen interactions directly or indirectly. By releasing [RNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rna "Learn more about RNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), pathogens can target a host's genes. They can also produce harmful proteins or interfere with the macrophage process. Some infections rely on elements of the host system to survive.

The chapter begins with a pathogen classification and then moves on to a section that looks at how host–pathogen interactions are defined using various criteria and biological reasoning. The methods of in silico prediction that are often utilized in the context of host–pathogen interactions are then explored. After that, we'll provide you with a detailed list of online archives. A quick assessment of the overall benefits and downsides of this study topic is provided at the chapter's conclusion, along with some last thoughts.

[Computational means to understand and elucidate host–pathogen interaction and outcome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780443187698000143)

[2023, Genomic Surveillance and Pandemic Preparedness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780443187698/genomic-surveillance-and-pandemic-preparedness)Preeti Agarwal, ... Jitendra Narayan

### Abstract

Host–pathogen interactions are a rapidly expanding and evolving field of study. A new infection is identified every other day, creating the dilemma of prevention and therapy. Understanding the mechanics of host–pathogen interactions becomes more important since a wise person will always choose prevention over treatment. When an interaction occurs, both the pathogen and the host are involved in a number of ways. It is a battle of opposing proteins and genes on both sides. Whether a host contracts an infection or not determines who wins. Naturally, as pathogens evolve and become capable of bypassing a host's [immune system](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/immune-system "Learn more about immune system from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), their complexity grows. These pathogens pose extremely complex treatment challenges. These chronic, long-term ailments endanger the entire human population. Even some of these infections are unconcerned about the time crunch. We discuss the computational and biological features of host–pathogen interactions, as well as categorizing the ways of how pathogens interact with their hosts, various machine learning algorithms currently being utilized for the prediction of host–pathogen interactions, and probable future avenues for this field of study in this chapter.

Chapter

[Protein-Protein Interactions: An Overview](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780128096338202926)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2019, Encyclopedia of Bioinformatics and Computational Biology](https://www.sciencedirect.com/referencework/9780128114322/encyclopedia-of-bioinformatics-and-computational-biology)Edson L. Folador, ... Vasco Azevedo

### Host-Pathogen Interaction

The PINs still provide the possibility to investigate host-pathogen interactions and can be applied to understand the relationship between pathogen and host at systems biology level as suggesting proteins or interactions as potential targets for drug. Aspects related to virulence such as adhesion, escape of the immune system, proliferation are often dependent on the host-pathogen interaction and the knowledge of these mechanisms, even partially, can be determinant to understand the infection and to guide new experiments.

In addition to intracellular PIN, should be considered spatio-temporal aspects of host-pathogen PIN prediction, that is, the proteins participants of interaction must coexist and must be in cellular location favoring interaction, being potential participants in host-pathogen interaction the membrane, transmembrane or secreted proteins. The identification of host-pathogen interaction mainly promotes research into new interaction-inhibitors drug preventing infection be harmful to the host.

The host-pathogen PINs were used to identify interactions among _[Mycobacterium tuberculosis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mycobacterium-tuberculosis "Learn more about Mycobacterium tuberculosis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ and _[Homo sapiens](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/human "Learn more about Homo sapiens from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ (Huo _et al._, 2015). Using the SVM method was predicted the PIN of viruses with _Homo sapiens_ as interactions of both [hepatitis C virus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/hepatitis-c-virus "Learn more about hepatitis C virus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [papillomavirus](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/papillomaviridae "Learn more about papillomavirus from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") against _humans_ obtaining 80% accuracy (Cui _et al._, 2012).

In the study between _Nipah_ virus and _human_ 101 interactions were identified, 88 never found previously, being the main targets the [miRNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/microrna "Learn more about miRNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") processing machinery and the preprocessing factor of mRNA PRP19, including proteins capable of altering the p53 gene expression (Martinez-Gil _et al._, 2017). Many targets for existing drugs are contained in a small number of families as [guanine](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/guanine "Learn more about guanine from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") nucleotide-binding protein (G-protein) – acute receptor, [nuclear hormone receptor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nuclear-hormone-receptor "Learn more about nuclear hormone receptor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), ionic channel kinase, and protease.

Chapter

[Experimental repertoire to elucidate the functional landscape of host–pathogen interactome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780443187698000039)
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2023, Genomic Surveillance and Pandemic Preparedness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780443187698/genomic-surveillance-and-pandemic-preparedness)Partha Chattopadhyay, ... Rajesh Pandey

### Experimental approach to understand host–pathogen interaction

There exists molecular cross-talks between hosts and their pathogens via proteins, [nucleic acids](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/nucleic-acid "Learn more about nucleic acids from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [small molecules](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/small-molecule "Learn more about small molecules from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which are the basis for all [biological functions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/biological-functions "Learn more about biological functions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Since these interactions ultimately decide the disease outcome and alter disease trajectory, understanding these host-pathogen interactions is essential for developing ground-breaking/innovative therapies to combat infectious diseases. Experimental, bioinformatics, and computational tools have made it attainable to study the biological pathways involved in pathogen replication, host response, and disease progression. The development of novel tools and methodologies has greatly contributed to our understanding of host–pathogen interactions mechanisms, including the microbial flow cytometry coupled to next-generation sequencing (mFLOW-SEQ), high-resolution mass spectrometry (MS)-based [proteomics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/proteomics "Learn more about proteomics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as well as conventional genomics approach (Mahmud and Garrett, 2020; Silverman and Green, 2021). The mFLOW-SEQ technique has enabled the identification of antibody-mediated, host-microbe interactions, wherein the host's antibodies are employed to detect the microbes recognized by the adaptive immune system (Silverman and Green, 2021).

The protein interactions between the pathogen and the host are critical for the replication and spread of the pathogen inside the host. Understanding these protein interactions might aid in treatments against the human infectious diseases. Proteomics methods are used to understand the host-pathogen interactions which occur regularly throughout the biological pathways, including the protein complement of the system (Jean Beltran et al., 2017). The MS-based proteomics allowed the elucidation of important aspects modulating host-pathogen interactions, and the pathogen mechanisms to circumvent the host defense. This host–pathogen protein–protein interactions (PPIs) can also be characterized via methods such as shotgun immuno-purification IP-MS, top-down [MS](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/mass-spectrometry "Learn more about MS from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), along with the classic yeast two-hybrid (Y2H) assay (Budayeva and Cristea, 2014; Greisch et al., 2021; Striebinger et al., 2013).

[2023, Genomic Surveillance and Pandemic Preparedness](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780443187698/genomic-surveillance-and-pandemic-preparedness)Preeti Agarwal, ... Jitendra Narayan

### Conclusion

A wide range of subjects relating to host–pathogen interactions has been discussed in this chapter. The way a virus interacts with its host or hosts is never the same. Every pathogenic species has a different manner of interacting with its host. This chapter examines the various techniques of various species, as well as the parallels and related features in pathogen-attacking systems (s). In the realm of host–pathogen interactions, it has listed prediction strategies based on biological reasoning, machine learning, and, in some circumstances, both methods. A variety of data sources were also discovered for this domain investigation. In the postgenomic age, host–pathogen interactions are emerging as an important topic in infectious disease research (Mehta et al., 2022). It is a growing scientific field, with new discoveries being made virtually every day all across the world. The discovery of host–pathogen interaction [dynamics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/immunology-and-microbiology/dynamics "Learn more about dynamics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") will greatly aid future progress in developing novel treatments and cures for a variety of disorders.

Chapter

[Molecular biology of antiviral arms race between plants and viruses](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780128216293000038)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2021, Plant Virus-Host Interaction (Second Edition)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780128216293/plant-virus-host-interaction)Devendran Ragunathan, ... R. Vinoth Kumar

### 1 Introduction

Host-pathogen interaction is a diverse process and this tug of war between them evolves endlessly. As plants are sessile in nature, they are constantly under attack by numerous threats by pathogens throughout its [life cycle](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/life-cycle "Learn more about life cycle from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Plants have developed efficient molecular mechanisms to recognize and respond to these invading pathogens during the course of evolution. Further, pathogens also evolve rapidly to evade this host-derived immune response to establish successful infections. Plant viruses induce physiological disorders which are termed as symptoms, which could probably result in severe crop losses. The resistant lines generated through conventional breeding are the main source of resistance against viruses but viruses are also equipped themselves to successfully break this host-mediated antiviral response. This necessitates to understand the molecular aspects of plant-virus interaction to devise any disease management strategies (Nicaise, 2014). The outcome of the past several years of research emphasizes the importance [RNA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/rna "Learn more about RNA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") silencing-mediated processes in providing immunity against viruses. Currently, several RNA silencing-based antiviral strategies are successfully employed as the potential tool for [crop protection](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/crop-protection "Learn more about crop protection from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") against plant viruses. This chapter discusses the RNA silencing-mediated mechanisms employed by the plants to defend itself from viruses, and the strategies devised by viruses to counter this defense processes. It also compiles the data on the implication of various RNA silencing-based antiviral strategies to combat several plant viral diseases.

Chapter

[Omics in tuber crops: Cassava and sweet potato](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/B9780323899055000215)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

[2022, Omics in Horticultural Crops](https://www.sciencedirect.com/book/9780323899055/omics-in-horticultural-crops)Sreekumar Janardanan, Sona Charles

### 8.2 Plant-pathogen interaction

Plants have a close relationship with microbes in the natural environment. Plants also possess a response mechanism against a pathogen attack through pattern-triggered immunity and effector-triggered immunity. Interaction of a pathogen with its host begins with recognition of the receptor on the host surface. Plant–pathogen interaction is a complex network of underlying molecular players from both pathogens and their host (Mukhtar et al., 2016). Host–pathogen interactions are dynamic evolutionary processes in which virulence and resistance can be altered by even a single [amino acid](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/amino-acids "Learn more about amino acid from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") change. The level of damage to the affected crops depends on the outcome of interactions, often reducing the fitness (Brown, 2015). To further study the mechanisms in host–pathogen interactions, it is important to integrate omics-based approaches combined with machine learning methods to push the limits in this area. Due to increased sequencing of various plant and pathogen genomes and emergence of long read, third-generation sequencing strategies at genome-level studies through network-based approaches have been fruitful model plants. Network analysis plays a pivotal role in understanding the comprehensive patterns of wiring of proteins after infection. Combining the advances in characterization of the cellular components operating in the [plant genome](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/plant-genome "Learn more about plant genome from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [transcriptomic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/transcriptomics "Learn more about transcriptomic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") data, plant pathogen interaction networks have been constructed in model-plants (Zhou and Zhang, 2020). Bacterial blight disease is caused by _[Xanthomonas axonopodis](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/biochemistry-genetics-and-molecular-biology/xanthomonas-axonopodis "Learn more about Xanthomonas axonopodis from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")_ in tuber crops. The difference in protein–protein interaction in defense mechanism against this pathogen was performed in two cultivars KU50 and AM56 (Thanasomboon et al., 2019).

enriched_points.expanded_content: Host-pathogen interactions are dynamic relationships where pathogens invade host organisms, influencing disease outcomes and immune responses at molecular and cellular levels.
Understanding host-pathogen interactions is crucial for developing effective treatments, vaccines, and prevention strategies against infectious diseases, thereby improving public health outcomes.
This report aims to explore the complexities of host-pathogen interactions, analyze their implications for disease progression, and identify potential therapeutic targets for intervention.
